sections.plain_summary: Gastroparesis is a condition where the stomach moves food into the small intestine more slowly than normal, even though there is no physical blockage. This delay can cause nausea, vomiting, bloating, and poor nutrition because food sits in the stomach too long.
sections.detailed_summary: Gastroparesis (literally 'stomach paralysis') occurs when the stomach's muscles don't contract properly to push food forward, or when the valve between the stomach and small intestine (pylorus) doesn't relax enough. The stomach empties more slowly than normal, causing food to remain in the stomach for hours. This can lead to symptoms like severe nausea, vomiting, early fullness, bloating, and abdominal pain. Over time, slow gastric emptying can result in malnutrition, dehydration, and blood sugar control problems. The condition may be triggered by diabetes, certain medications, vagus nerve damage, or gastric surgery, but in many cases the cause is unknown (idiopathic gastroparesis).

prevalence.summary: Gastroparesis affects roughly 1-4 people per 100,000 each year, though the true number may be higher because many cases go undiagnosed.
prevalence.estimate: 1–4 per 100,000 population per year; chronic prevalence estimated at 0.1–0.3%
prevalence.notes: Estimates vary widely because symptoms overlap with other conditions and diagnosis requires specialized testing. Diabetes is the most common known cause, and diabetic gastroparesis may be more common than idiopathic forms. Women are diagnosed more often than men, possibly due to better symptom reporting or true sex-based differences in prevalence. Many patients suffer for years before diagnosis.

subgroups.name: Diabetic gastroparesis
subgroups.description: Occurs in people with type 1 or type 2 diabetes, caused by high blood sugar damaging the vagus nerve and stomach muscles over time.
distinguishing_features: Often develops after many years of diabetes
May improve with tight blood sugar control
Symptoms may fluctuate with blood glucose levels
More common and may progress more slowly than idiopathic form

subgroups.name: Idiopathic gastroparesis
subgroups.description: No identifiable cause can be found despite testing; sometimes follows a viral illness.
distinguishing_features: Often more severe and harder to treat than diabetic form
More common in young to middle-aged women
Diagnosis requires exclusion of other causes

subgroups.name: Post-surgical gastroparesis
subgroups.description: Develops after gastric surgery (bariatric, ulcer repair, or other abdominal procedures), usually temporary but can persist.
distinguishing_features: Often resolves within weeks to months
May be related to vagus nerve injury or scarring

subgroups.name: Medication-induced gastroparesis
subgroups.description: Caused by medications that slow stomach contractions, such as opioids, certain antipsychotics, or anticholinergics.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms improve when the offending medication is stopped or reduced
Generally reversible if the drug is discontinued
Opioids are the most common culprit

symptoms.notes: Often the primary symptom; may be worse after eating. Persistent and can be severe enough to interfere with daily life.
Occurs when the stomach cannot empty food normally. May happen hours after eating and lead to dehydration and electrolyte loss.
Eating even small amounts of food causes a sense of fullness and bloating, making adequate nutrition difficult.
Occurs as food accumulates and gas builds up in the stomach.
Can range from mild cramping to severe pain, often epigastric (upper abdomen).
Often due to nausea and discomfort rather than true appetite suppression.

symptoms.symptom: Weight loss or failure to gain weight
symptoms.notes: Results from inadequate food intake and poor nutrient absorption when meals are small and infrequent.
Chronic undereating can lead to deficiencies in protein, iron, calcium, and vitamins.

symptoms.symptom: Blood sugar swings or worsening diabetes control
symptoms.notes: Unpredictable gastric emptying makes it difficult to time insulin or predict blood glucose response to meals.
May result from poor nutrition, chronic nausea, or metabolic disruption.
Caused by food sitting in the stomach and fermenting due to slow emptying.

key_patterns: Persistent nausea and early fullness after eating, even small meals
Symptoms that worsen after fatty or high-fiber foods
Weight loss or difficulty maintaining weight despite adequate calorie intake attempts
Symptoms lasting weeks or months, not improving with lifestyle changes alone
A clear trigger event such as surgery, diabetes diagnosis, or acute illness before symptoms began
Symptoms that do not respond to standard antacid or acid-reflux medications

at_home.name: Food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Record everything eaten (type, amount, fat content, fiber), the time eaten, and the timing and severity of nausea, fullness, vomiting, or bloating for 1–2 weeks. Note any patterns (e.g., symptoms always appear 3 hours after a large meal, or after fatty foods).
at_home.safety_notes: This is observational and safe for all. It helps you and your doctor identify patterns but does not diagnose gastroparesis.

at_home.name: Dietary trial: small, frequent, low-fat meals
at_home.description: For 1–2 weeks, eat 5–6 small meals per day with minimal fat and fiber. Note whether symptoms improve. Gastroparesis typically responds to smaller portions; other conditions may not.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe and non-invasive. If symptoms worsen or you develop malnutrition signs (dizziness, severe fatigue), return to normal eating and seek professional evaluation.

clinical.name: Gastric emptying scintigraphy (radionuclide scintigraphy)
clinical.description: The patient eats a small amount of food tagged with a radioactive tracer (usually a low-dose technetium-99m), and a gamma camera tracks how quickly the stomach empties over 1–4 hours. Delayed emptying confirms gastroparesis. This is the most specific diagnostic test.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologists or primary care doctors; performed at hospital or imaging centers with nuclear medicine capability.

clinical.name: Wireless motility capsule
clinical.description: Patient swallows a small capsule that measures stomach contractions and transit time as it moves through the digestive tract. Data is recorded wirelessly. Less radiation than scintigraphy but more invasive.
clinical.availability: Specialized gastroenterology centers; less widely available than scintigraphy.

clinical.name: Antral manometry (electrogastrography or high-resolution gastric pressure topography)
clinical.description: A thin tube with pressure sensors is passed into the stomach to directly measure muscle contractions and pyloric sphincter function. Identifies abnormal contractions or delayed emptying.
clinical.availability: Specialized centers with motility expertise; less common than scintigraphy.

clinical.name: Upper endoscopy (EGD)
clinical.description: A thin camera is passed into the stomach to directly visualize the stomach lining and look for obstructions, ulcers, or other blockages. Does not measure emptying rate but rules out mechanical causes.
clinical.availability: Standard procedure available at most hospitals and endoscopy centers; often done as first step to exclude obstruction.

clinical.name: CT or MRI of the abdomen
clinical.description: Imaging to rule out mechanical obstruction, scarring, or other structural abnormalities that could mimic gastroparesis.
clinical.availability: Widely available; typically ordered to exclude other causes.

clinical.name: Breath testing (13C octanoic acid breath test)
clinical.description: Patient ingests food labeled with non-radioactive stable isotope (13C). As the stomach empties, the tracer is absorbed and metabolized, appearing in exhaled breath. Less radiation than scintigraphy; more convenient but less widely validated.
clinical.availability: Available in some specialized centers; not yet standard in all countries.

related_conditions.name: Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic hyperglycemia damages the vagus nerve and stomach muscle, leading to gastroparesis in up to 30% of long-term diabetic patients. Conversely, gastroparesis worsens blood sugar control by making meal timing unpredictable.

related_conditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
related_conditions.explanation: Slow gastric emptying can promote acid reflux because food and acid remain in the stomach longer. Conversely, severe GERD symptoms may be mistaken for gastroparesis.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Both cause postprandial fullness, bloating, and nausea. Functional dyspepsia is diagnosed when upper endoscopy and gastric emptying studies are normal; the two can coexist.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Overlapping symptoms of nausea, bloating, and pain. Some patients have both slow gastric emptying and disturbed small bowel motility; the conditions may share underlying neuromotor dysfunction.

related_conditions.name: Eating disorders (anorexia nervosa, bulimia)
related_conditions.explanation: Restrictive eating and purging can slow gastric emptying (secondary gastroparesis), and symptoms may overlap. Distinguishing primary gastroparesis from eating disorder-related dysfunction requires careful history and psychological assessment.

related_conditions.name: Parkinson's disease
related_conditions.explanation: Dopamine dysfunction affects both the brain and gastrointestinal motility. Patients with Parkinson's are at higher risk for gastroparesis.

related_conditions.name: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma)
related_conditions.explanation: Fibrosis and smooth muscle dysfunction in scleroderma can impair gastric motility, leading to secondary gastroparesis.

related_conditions.name: Migraine disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Both gastroparesis and migraines involve dysmotility and neuronal dysregulation. Patients with migraines have higher rates of gastroparesis, and migraine medications (e.g., sumatriptan) can affect gastric motility.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety and depression
related_conditions.explanation: Psychological stress and negative affect can worsen gastric motility and symptom perception. Gastroparesis can also cause or exacerbate anxiety and depression due to chronic illness burden.

strategies.name: Small, frequent, low-fat meals
strategies.description: Eat 5–6 small meals per day instead of 3 large ones. Keep each meal under 500 kcal and fat under 30% of calories. Small meals are easier for the impaired stomach to move forward; low fat reduces the stimulus for delayed emptying (fat is the slowest macronutrient to empty).
strategies.notes: Foundational first-line approach. Individual tolerance varies; some people do better with even smaller portions. Monitor weight and nutrition; may need supplements if eating becomes too restricted.

strategies.name: Low-fiber diet
strategies.description: Limit high-fiber foods (whole grains, raw vegetables, beans) in favor of refined carbs, well-cooked vegetables, and processed foods. Fiber can mass in the stomach and slow emptying further.
strategies.notes: Temporary measure for symptom relief; long-term low-fiber eating may reduce beneficial microbiota. Gradually reintroduce fiber as symptoms improve.

strategies.name: Liquid and semi-liquid meals
strategies.description: Soups, smoothies, yogurt, applesauce, pudding, and broths are easier to empty than solid foods. Blending solid foods increases surface area and speeds digestion.
strategies.notes: Very helpful for acute symptom relief. Can be harder to meet caloric and nutrient needs with liquids alone; may need nutritional supplements.

strategies.name: Metoclopramide
strategies.description: A dopamine antagonist that enhances stomach muscle contractions and improves gastric emptying. Typical dose is 10 mg three times daily before meals.
strategies.notes: Most commonly prescribed medication for gastroparesis. Long-term use (>3 months) carries a small risk of tardive dyskinesia (involuntary movements). Regular reassessment is recommended; some patients develop tolerance. Not suitable for those with prolactin-sensitive tumors or certain psychiatric conditions.

strategies.name: Domperidone
strategies.description: Another dopamine antagonist, works similarly to metoclopramide but does not cross the blood-brain barrier as much, so lower risk of CNS side effects. Available in many countries outside the US.
strategies.notes: Not FDA-approved in the US but widely used elsewhere. May be obtained through compassionate use programs. Similar efficacy to metoclopramide with potentially fewer neurological side effects.

strategies.name: Domperidone
strategies.description: Another dopamine antagonist, works similarly to metoclopramide but does not cross the blood-brain barrier as much, so lower risk of CNS side effects. Available in many countries outside the US.
strategies.notes: Not FDA-approved in the US but widely used elsewhere. May be obtained through compassionate use programs. Similar efficacy to metoclopramide with potentially fewer neurological side effects.

strategies.name: Prucalopride
strategies.description: A selective serotonin 5-HT4 agonist that enhances acetylcholine release and improves gastric contractions. Emerging option with possibly fewer long-term side effects than dopamine antagonists.
strategies.notes: Evidence base is growing but smaller than for metoclopramide. May be offered in specialized centers or as a second-line option if metoclopramide fails or is not tolerated.

strategies.name: Ginger supplementation
strategies.description: Ginger root (fresh, powder, or extract, typically 1–2 g daily) may enhance gastric contractions and reduce nausea through multiple mechanisms.
strategies.notes: More evidence for nausea relief than for improved gastric emptying. Generally safe and well-tolerated; can be combined with medications. Effects are modest and variable.

strategies.name: Peppermint oil
strategies.description: Enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules may reduce bloating and abdominal pain by relaxing smooth muscle, though effects on gastric emptying are unclear.
strategies.notes: Primarily helpful for symptom comfort rather than addressing delayed emptying. Well-tolerated; do not use if acid reflux is severe.

strategies.name: Acupuncture
strategies.description: Traditional acupuncture or electroacupuncture applied to specific points (e.g., ST36, CV12) may stimulate gastric contractions and improve symptoms in some patients.
strategies.notes: Limited high-quality evidence. Some patients report symptom relief; effects are variable. May be worth trying if conventional treatments are ineffective or poorly tolerated.

strategies.name: Eating position and pacing
strategies.description: Eat upright (not reclined) and chew thoroughly. Slow eating allows better sensory feedback and may reduce symptom perception. Remain upright for 30 minutes after eating to aid gravity-assisted emptying.
strategies.notes: Simple behavioral change with evidence support. Particularly helpful when combined with other strategies.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and relaxation
strategies.description: Techniques like deep breathing, meditation, yoga, or progressive muscle relaxation can reduce the negative impact of stress and anxiety on stomach motility.
strategies.notes: Psychological stress worsens symptoms. Regular practice improves both subjective symptoms and, in some cases, measurable gastric function.

strategies.name: Warm liquids and soups
strategies.description: Warm foods and broths may aid gastric relaxation and comfort, and provide calories and hydration.
strategies.notes: Subjective preference varies. Avoid very hot liquids if reflux is present.

strategies.name: Nutritional supplementation
strategies.description: If oral intake is insufficient, liquid nutrition supplements (e.g., Ensure, Boost) or micronutrient supplements (iron, B12, calcium) may prevent deficiencies.
strategies.notes: Essential if solid food intake is severely limited. Consider with a dietitian to tailor to individual needs. In severe cases, tube feeding or parenteral nutrition may be required.

strategies.name: Avoid or minimize opioid medications
strategies.description: Opioids slow gastric emptying and worsen gastroparesis. If pain control is needed, discuss non-opioid alternatives with your doctor.
strategies.notes: Crucial intervention if opioids are being used. Even small doses can have a significant impact on motility.

things_to_avoid: High-fat foods (fried foods, butter, cream, fatty meats) — fat is the slowest macronutrient to empty and triggers delayed emptying
High-fiber foods, especially raw vegetables and whole grains — fiber can mass in the stomach and obstruct flow
Large meals — overwhelm the weakened stomach and prolong emptying; multiple small meals are better tolerated
Carbonated drinks and artificial sweeteners — gas and sugar alcohols worsen bloating and discomfort
Alcohol and caffeine — both can irritate the stomach and impair motility
Spicy or high-fat condiments (hot sauce, mayonnaise, creamy dressings) — can trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals
Opioid pain relievers — directly slow gastric emptying and worsen the condition
Anticholinergic medications (antihistamines like diphenhydramine, certain antidepressants) — reduce stomach contractions
Lying down immediately after eating — gravity assists emptying; recumbency promotes reflux and delays gastric transit

key_levers.lever: Establishing a consistent pattern of small (300–400 kcal), low-fat (<10 g), refined-carb meals every 2–3 hours
key_levers.why_it_matters: This is the single most impactful dietary modification because it directly addresses the core problem (stomach's reduced ability to handle volume and fat). Consistency allows the stomach to anticipate and process meals more smoothly.
key_levers.how_to_start: Map out 5 small meals a day for a week: breakfast, mid-morning snack, lunch, afternoon snack, early dinner. Use simple, well-tolerated foods (plain chicken, white rice, bananas, applesauce). Track how you feel after each meal.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom relief often begins within days to 1–2 weeks; more complete stabilization may take 4–8 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: High blood glucose directly impairs stomach muscle function and vagal signaling. Even modest improvements in A1C can meaningfully improve gastric emptying and gastroparesis symptoms.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with your diabetes care team to review and optimize insulin timing, doses, and medication regimen. More frequent blood glucose monitoring or continuous glucose monitor (CGM) use can help you identify patterns between glucose levels and symptoms.
key_levers.timeline: Improvements in gastric function may take weeks to months as nerve and muscle function gradually recover with sustained better control.

key_levers.lever: Discontinuing or switching away from medications that impair motility (opioids, anticholinergics, certain antipsychotics)
key_levers.why_it_matters: These medications directly slow stomach contractions and can fully reverse gastroparesis symptoms once withdrawn. This is one of the highest-impact interventions when applicable.
key_levers.how_to_start: Review your medication list with your doctor. If you're on opioids for pain, discuss non-opioid alternatives (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, topical agents, physical therapy, or pain psychology). Never stop medications suddenly without medical guidance.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvement can begin within days after stopping the culprit drug.

key_levers.lever: Medication optimization: starting or adjusting prokinetic agents (metoclopramide, prucalopride, domperidone)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Prokinetics directly enhance stomach contractions and accelerate gastric emptying, often providing significant symptom relief and allowing a broader diet.
key_levers.how_to_start: If dietary changes alone are insufficient after 2–4 weeks, discuss with your gastroenterologist or primary care doctor. Metoclopramide is most commonly prescribed; prucalopride or domperidone may be options if first-line agents fail.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom relief often begins within 1–2 weeks; full benefit may take 4 weeks. Tolerance can develop over months to years, so regular reassessment is important.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The gut-brain axis means stress and negative affect directly worsen gastric motility and symptom perception. Stress reduction improves both measurable gastric function and subjective symptom burden.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify a stress-reduction practice that fits your life: daily 10-minute meditation, yoga, therapy, or even a daily walk. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) for health anxiety or medical trauma is particularly helpful.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvement often appears within 2–4 weeks of consistent practice; sustained benefit builds over months.

key_levers.lever: Nutritional assessment and supplementation to prevent deficiencies
key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic undereating leads to protein, iron, B12, calcium, and micronutrient deficiencies, which worsen fatigue, weakness, and symptom perception. Correcting deficiencies improves energy and resilience.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask for a referral to a dietitian experienced in gastroparesis. Get baseline labs (complete blood count, iron panel, B12, folate, calcium, albumin, pre-albumin) to identify gaps. Use the results to guide targeted supplementation.
key_levers.timeline: Laboratory markers improve over weeks to months; symptom improvement (reduced fatigue, better energy) often appears within 4–12 weeks of correction.

common_mistakes: Attempting a normal diet too soon or eating large meals — leads to repeated nausea and vomiting cycles that reinforce the condition and worsen anxiety
Restricting calories to the point of malnutrition — attempting to 'eat less to feel less' backfires by reducing energy and worsening nutrient deficiencies
Stopping medications prematurely without medical guidance — symptoms often rebound, and some medications (especially antidepressants) require slow tapering
Ignoring blood sugar control if diabetic — high glucose perpetuates the underlying nerve and muscle damage
Continuing opioid use 'because you need them for pain' — opioids directly worsen gastroparesis and create a vicious cycle
Over-relying on anti-nausea medications (ondansetron) without addressing root causes — masks symptoms but does not improve gastric function or nutrition

Delaying diagnosis and treatment for years in hopes it will resolve on its own — idiopathic gastroparesis often requires professional intervention; early diagnosis prevents malnutrition and psychological burden
Dismissing the psychological toll — chronic gastroparesis is genuinely burdensome; ignoring anxiety and depression worsens both motility and quality of life

specialists.role: The primary specialist for diagnosis and medical management. Performs upper endoscopy to exclude obstruction, orders gastric emptying studies, and manages medication trials. Often the first point of contact.
specialists.notes: Seek a gastroenterologist with experience in motility disorders if possible; not all general gastroenterologists have deep expertise in gastroparesis.

specialists.type: Registered Dietitian (RD) with expertise in GI or gastroparesis
specialists.role: Essential for developing and refining a personalized eating plan, ensuring adequate nutrition despite dietary restrictions, and monitoring for deficiencies. Helps with meal planning and troubleshooting.
specialists.notes: Request a referral from your gastroenterologist or primary care doctor. Dietitians trained in gastroparesis know how to balance symptom control with nutrition—generic low-fiber diets often fall short.

specialists.type: Primary care physician (MD, DO, or NP)
specialists.role: Often the first to hear symptoms and can provide initial evaluation, order tests, start dietary counseling, and manage comorbidities (diabetes, anxiety). Can coordinate referrals.
specialists.notes: Some primary care doctors are unfamiliar with gastroparesis; if your doctor dismisses symptoms, ask for a gastroenterology referral.

specialists.role: Offers specialized expertise in complex cases, access to advanced testing (high-resolution manometry, wireless capsule), and more sophisticated pharmacological management (e.g., prucalopride trials).
specialists.notes: Usually found at tertiary care centers or specialized motility clinics. Worth seeking if initial treatment fails or symptoms are severe.

specialists.role: Helps optimize diabetes control, which directly improves gastroparesis. Works with primary care and gastroenterology to coordinate care.
specialists.notes: Especially important if your gastroparesis is diabetic and blood sugar control is suboptimal.

specialists.role: Helps identify non-opioid pain control strategies for patients with comorbid pain conditions who must avoid opioids.
specialists.notes: Useful if you have chronic pain and gastroparesis; can offer interventional techniques, non-opioid medications, or behavioral strategies.

specialists.role: Addresses anxiety, depression, medical trauma, and eating-related psychological issues that develop with or exacerbate gastroparesis. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is particularly useful.
specialists.notes: Gastroparesis is psychologically burdensome; therapy is not 'optional' for many patients and can significantly improve both mental health and symptom perception.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative medicine specialist
specialists.role: May offer additional perspectives on diet, supplements, stress reduction, and underlying causes. Some have specific interest in motility disorders.
specialists.notes: Approach with discernment; functional medicine is less regulated. Look for practitioners with clear evidence-based recommendations and collaboration with conventional gastroenterology.

patient_communities.name: American Motility Society
patient_communities.notes: Professional organization with some patient education and specialist locator. Credible and evidence-based.

patient_communities.name: Gastroparesis Support Community (Facebook)
patient_communities.notes: Large peer support group where patients share experiences, tips, medication side effects, and emotional support. Helpful for finding sympathy and practical advice, but verify medical claims with your doctor.

patient_communities.name: International Foundational Motility Disorders Organization (IFMDO)
patient_communities.notes: International patient and professional organization. Publishes guidelines, patient education, and curates specialist directories by region.

patient_communities.name: Rare Digestive Diseases Network (RDDN)
patient_communities.notes: A network for rare and underdiagnosed GI conditions, including some gastric motility disorders. Provides resources and research updates.

patient_communities.name: Reddit r/gastroparesis
patient_communities.notes: Active community for symptom discussion, medication experiences, and emotional support. Diverse perspectives; moderated for accuracy.

learning_resources.title: American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) Gastroparesis Guidelines
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based clinical guidelines for diagnosis and management of gastroparesis. Dense but authoritative; useful for patients who want to understand the medical evidence.

learning_resources.title: Gastroparesis: An Overview (Medscape)
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive but accessible medical overview covering etiology, pathophysiology, clinical presentation, diagnosis, and management. More detailed than typical patient resources but written for healthcare providers and educated patients.

learning_resources.title: What I Wish I Knew About Gastroparesis: A Patient Guide
learning_resources.description: Written by and for patients; covers symptoms, diagnosis, treatment options, dietary strategies, and living with gastroparesis. Practical and validating.

learning_resources.title: Gastroparesis: Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment (Mayo Clinic)
learning_resources.description: Clear, well-organized patient-friendly overview from a trusted institution. Good for understanding basics and when to seek care.

learning_resources.title: Living with Gastroparesis: A Diet and Lifestyle Guide
learning_resources.description: Practical guide with meal planning ideas, recipes, symptom management strategies, and emotional support. Often available through patient organizations or gastroenterology practices.

learning_resources.title: The Mind-Gut Connection (podcast or book excerpt)
learning_resources.description: Explores the gut-brain axis and how stress, anxiety, and psychological factors influence GI function. Helpful context for understanding why stress management matters in gastroparesis.

learning_resources.title: Idiopathic Gastroparesis: Clinical Heterogeneity and Management Strategies
learning_resources.author_or_source: Gastroparesis Research Foundation or journals like Neurogastroenterology & Motility
learning_resources.description: Peer-reviewed article diving into the heterogeneity of idiopathic gastroparesis and why treatment outcomes vary. Good for understanding why your experience may differ from others'.

learning_resources.title: Metoclopramide and Tardive Dyskinesia: A Review and Alternatives
learning_resources.description: Discusses the long-term risk of tardive dyskinesia with metoclopramide use, newer prokinetic options, and strategies for risk mitigation. Important for informed decision-making about long-term medication use.

learning_resources.title: Gastroparesis Patient Education Series (Video)
learning_resources.author_or_source: American Gastroenterological Association or university GI centers
learning_resources.description: Short, animated explainers of gastroparesis mechanisms, tests, and treatment. Often free on hospital or organization websites.

sections.relevance_signal: **Feeling full very quickly**, nausea after meals, upper abdominal bloating that starts during eating rather than hours later. Worse with fatty meals.
